description: Fix E2E test reliability issues in strict-csp, lifecycle, and browser-tools test files: resource leaks from missing try/finally, fixed sleeps instead of polling, and race conditions in WebSocket frame buffer verification.

userStories.title: Fix temp directory leaks in strict-csp.e2e.ts multi-plugin tests
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want temp directories created by createMinimalPlugin in strict-csp tests to be cleaned up on failure so that CI disk space is not consumed by leaked temp dirs on test failure paths.
acceptanceCriteria: The three multi-plugin tests in the 'Multi-plugin injection under strict CSP' describe block wrap their test body after tmpDir creation in try/finally to ensure fs.rmSync(tmpDir, ...) runs regardless of test outcome
Test at line ~591 ('two plugins both inject into a page with strict CSP') has try/finally around cleanup
Test at line ~653 ('e2e-test tool dispatch works on strict-CSP page with two adapters present') has try/finally around cleanup
Test at line ~718 ('strict CSP page with connect-src none: fetch-based tools fail but adapter survives') has try/finally around cleanup if it also uses createMinimalPlugin
Existing test behavior and assertions are unchanged
userStories.notes:
File: e2e/strict-csp.e2e.ts

Three tests in the 'Multi-plugin injection under strict CSP' describe block create temp directories via fs.mkdtempSync() for minimal plugins. The cleanup (fs.rmSync) is at the end of the test body but NOT inside try/finally. If any assertion or await between creation and cleanup throws, the temp directory leaks.

Pattern to fix (each test):
1. Find the `const tmpDir = fs.mkdtempSync(...)` line
2. Wrap everything from the next line through the `fs.rmSync(tmpDir, ...)` in try/finally
3. Move `fs.rmSync(tmpDir, { recursive: true, force: true })` into the finally block

Note: `page.close()` calls can stay outside the try/finally since the page is owned by the fixture and cleaned up automatically. The critical thing is the tmpDir.

Look at how the third test (around line 718) handles the same pattern — it may or may not also need fixing.

userStories.title: Replace fixed 5-second sleep with polling in lifecycle.e2e.ts URL change reconnection test
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want the lifecycle reconnection test to use polling instead of a fixed 5-second sleep so that the test is reliable under CI load and doesn't waste time on fast machines.
acceptanceCriteria: The fixed `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 5_000))` at line ~770 in the 'URL change reconnection' test is replaced with a polling-based wait
The replacement waits for the extension to be disconnected using waitForExtensionDisconnected or an equivalent polling helper
The test still verifies the same reconnection behavior (disconnect detected, port changed, reconnect succeeds)
userStories.notes:
File: e2e/lifecycle.e2e.ts, line ~770

The test kills server A and then does `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 5_000))` to wait for the extension to detect the disconnect and exhaust reconnect attempts. This fixed sleep is flaky under CI load (5s may not be enough) and wastes time on fast machines.

The helpers.ts file exports `waitForExtensionDisconnected(server, timeoutMs)` which polls /health until extensionConnected === false. However, the server is already dead here, so /health won't respond.

Better approach: After killing server A, poll the extension's service worker or health endpoint to verify the disconnect was detected. Since the server is dead, we can't use waitForExtensionDisconnected on server A. Instead, the point of the wait is to ensure the extension has given up its initial reconnect backoff. A practical replacement:
1. Use `waitForExtensionDisconnected(mcpServer, 10_000)` — even though server A is dead, the health check for server A will always return null (which means extensionConnected is false by default). Actually, re-read how waitForExtensionDisconnected works — it calls server.waitForHealth which needs the server alive.
2. Alternative: After killing server A, simply wait for a new server B to start, then use `waitForExtensionConnected(serverB)` after the port change. The 5-second wait is to let the extension exhaust retries before changing the port — but this may not be strictly necessary. Read the full test (lines 740-840) to understand the reconnection sequence and determine if the sleep can be replaced with polling on server B's health instead.

The key insight: the fixed sleep is between killing server A and starting server B. Its purpose is to ensure the extension is in a disconnected state before we change the port. Replace with: start server B immediately, change port, then let `waitForExtensionConnected(serverB)` handle the full wait. If the extension is still retrying server A, the port change will redirect it to B.

userStories.title: Fix race condition in WebSocket frame buffer clear verification in browser-tools.e2e.ts
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want the WebSocket frame buffer clear test to be robust against new frames arriving between the clear operation and the verification read so that the test doesn't produce flaky false negatives.
acceptanceCriteria: The WebSocket frame buffer 'clear' test (in the browser_get_websocket_frames describe block) no longer asserts the frame count is exactly 0 after clearing

Instead, the test either: (a) closes the WebSocket page before clearing to prevent new frames, or (b) uses a polling approach that accounts for new frames, or (c) navigates away from the WebSocket page before the clear+verify sequence
The test still verifies that the clear operation removes previously captured frames
All other WebSocket frame tests in the same describe block continue to pass
userStories.notes:
File: e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts, in the browser_get_websocket_frames describe block

The test:
1. Opens /ws-test page which establishes a live WebSocket connection
2. Captures frames via browser_get_websocket_frames
3. Calls browser_get_websocket_frames with { clear: true } — returns current frames and clears the buffer
4. Immediately calls browser_get_websocket_frames again and asserts frame count is 0

The race: The WebSocket connection is still alive at step 4. The echo server sends messages back, so new frames can arrive between step 3 (clear) and step 4 (verify). This causes an intermittent failure where the frame count is 1 or 2 instead of 0.

Fix approach: Navigate away from the /ws-test page (or close it) between steps 3 and 4 to sever the WebSocket connection and prevent new frames. Alternatively, disable network capture before the verification read, then re-enable it. The simplest fix is to navigate the tab to about:blank (which closes the WebSocket) before the verification read, then check the buffer is empty.
